scenario: Umbra Bank ships a small Camunda 8.8 worker in Node.js for a back-office task that runs about 80 jobs per day. A new contributor asks which JavaScript SDK the Writing good workers page recommends for the example Node.js worker that uses an HTTP library such as Axios.
question: Which Node.js SDK does the Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers page name as the basis for its Node.js example?

options.text: The Camunda 8 JavaScript SDK published under github.com/camunda/camunda-8-js-sdk is the example library, and any HTTP library such as Axios may be used alongside it for outbound REST calls from the worker code

A community Node.js wrapper around the Zeebe gRPC API is the example library, with HTTP calls performed exclusively through the Node.js fetch API since Axios is described as removed in 8.8 in Camunda 8.8
The Camunda 7 External Task Client for Node.js is the example library, used together with Express to expose worker endpoints to the broker via webhooks in Camunda 8.8
There is no Node.js example in Writing good workers; the page redirects Node developers to call the Camunda 8 REST APIs directly from their own HTTP stack without any SDK at all

a.text: Correct. The Node.js client section of Writing good workers links to the camunda-8-js-sdk repository as the basis for the Node.js worker example, and notes that Axios is the assumed HTTP library while any other HTTP library is also fine.

b.text: Incorrect. The page points to the official camunda-8-js-sdk, not a community gRPC wrapper, and does not describe Axios as deprecated.

c.text: Incorrect. The page references the Camunda 8 JavaScript SDK and not a Camunda 7 external-task client, and Express + webhooks is not the pattern shown.

d.text: Incorrect. A Node.js example is present and is based on the camunda-8-js-sdk.

explanation: The Node.js example in Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers is built on the official camunda-8-js-sdk and may use any HTTP library, with Axios used as the illustrative choice in the snippet.
